name: Jairus' daughter and the woman with the issue of blood
notes: A threefold synoptic retelling in which one miracle is nested inside another: a ruler comes to Jesus on behalf of his dying daughter, and on the way a woman with a twelve-year issue of blood is healed by touching his garment, after which the maid is raised. Mark and Luke name the ruler Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue, and supply the fuller account of the woman's many physicians, Jesus' question 'Who touched me,' and the words 'Talitha cumi'; Matthew compresses the whole, calling him 'a certain ruler' and reporting the daughter as already dead at his coming. The shared core is firm: the ruler falling at Jesus' feet, the press of the throng, the hem of the garment, 'thy faith hath made thee whole,' the message that the damsel is dead, 'the maid is not dead, but sleepeth,' the scornful laughter, the taking by the hand, her arising, and the report going abroad. Where Matthew abbreviates and Mark and Luke expand, the accounts give complementary vantages of the same day; Matthew's 'even now dead' set beside Mark and Luke's 'at the point of death' / 'lay a dying' is left as a matter for further study. Mark and Luke's unique fuller detail (the physicians, the woman's trembling confession, 'Be not afraid, only believe,' Talitha cumi, the three disciples) reads in place, unlinked where not genuinely shared.
